methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $183.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $1.0B - $126.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Together AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series G), post-money $3.3B

private.name: Labelbox
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series G), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Anthropic, a Series G AI & Software company founded by Dario and Daniela Amodei, carries a current post-money valuation of $183.0B following $13B+ in total fundraising. The peer universe comprises five same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables spanning late-stage and Series F/G funding rounds, with valuations ranging from $1.0B (Labelbox) to $126.0B (Waymo).

Two valuation methods prove applicable. Stage-progression analysis applies a typical 1.25x step-up multiplier from Series G, implying $228.8B. Peer-median triangulation across five AI & Software comparables yields $4.1B, though this reflects material dispersion: the five-company median masks a $1.0B–$126.0B range, indicating heterogeneous risk and market positioning within the AI sector.

The triangulated analysis supports a range of $3.5B (low) to $263.1B (high), with a median of $116.4B. The wide band reflects acute tension between stage-progression guidance and peer-relative valuation. At $183.0B, Anthropic trades substantially above the peer median of $4.1B, implying either premium positioning relative to comparables or limited comparability given the company's capital intensity, product differentiation, and investor demand in frontier AI. Key uncertainties include comparable company selection methodology, the relevance of Waymo's $126B valuation to large-language-model companies, and whether Series G peers adequately reflect Anthropic's scale and capital requirements.
